entries.title: Arbitrary Tailwind value blocking to enforce token-only spacing
entries.description:
# Arbitrary Tailwind value blocking to enforce token-only spacing

## Context

Captured during development session on 2026-02-21

## Pattern

The forbidden-primitive-tokens rule in chains/rules/enforcement.yaml blocks arbitrary Tailwind values like w-[200px], p-[15px], shadow-[...], and !important. These bypass the design token system and create inconsistent spacing/sizing. Instead, use named Tailwind classes from the design token scale (p-2, gap-4, w-full) which map to the two-layer spacing system (4pt precision + 8pt rhythm). The rule fires as a blocking action on .tsx files.

## Example

```typescript
Blocked: className='w-[200px] p-[15px]'. Fix: className='w-48 p-4' (uses token scale).
```

## Why

Arbitrary values create visual inconsistency and break theme portability. Token-only spacing ensures all components align to the same grid.

entries.title: Two-Layer Spacing System (4pt Precision + 8pt Rhythm)
entries.description:
# Two-Layer Spacing System ## Context Spacing inconsistency is one of the most common design system violations. Teams often debate "4pt grid vs 8pt grid" as if they're mutually exclusive. The solution is not choosing one — it's assigning responsibility to each. ## Pattern **One scale, two layers.** Use a single unified scale with role-based layer assignment: **Scale:** `4 · 8 · 12 · 16 · 20 · 24 · 32 · 40 · 48` **4pt precision layer** — handles detail: - Icons and text alignment (4px gap) - Typography baseline adjustments (4px increments) - Internal component spacing (12px, 20px) - Fine-grained padding where 8pt is too coarse **8pt rhythm layer** — creates rhythm: - Layout-level spacing (16px, 24px, 32px) - Component padding and margins - Section spacing (40px, 48px) - Page-level divisions **Baseline grid:** Typography must snap to the 8pt baseline grid. Line-heights should be multiples of 8px (16px, 24px, 32px) so text aligns across columns and components. **Philosophy:** Parsed, not guessed. Spacing values must be enforced programmatically — never eyeballed. ## Example ```tsx // CORRECT: Values from the scale, layers respected {/* 24px: rhythm layer */}
{/* 4px: precision layer (icon-text) */} Label
{/* 16px: rhythm layer (content gap) */}

{/* 24px line-height: snaps to 8pt baseline */} Content text here

// WRONG: Random off-grid values {/* 18px: OFF GRID */}
{/* 5px: OFF GRID */} Label
{/* 29px: OFF GRID */}

{/* 15px: OFF BASELINE GRID */} Content text here

``` ## Why - **Consistent grid creates clarity** — users perceive visual harmony even if they can't articulate why - **Random values confuse users** — 5px, 7px, 18px, 29px create subtle visual noise that erodes trust - **Two layers prevent the false choice** — 4pt and 8pt serve different roles, not competing philosophies - **Baseline grid anchors typography** — text that snaps to the grid feels intentional and aligned - **Parsed, not guessed** — programmatic enforcement catches violations that the human eye misses

entries.title: Token enforcement pyramid: blocked → forbidden → preferred layers
entries.description:
# Token enforcement pyramid: blocked → forbidden → preferred layers

## Context

Captured during development session on 2026-02-21

## Pattern

Salvador enforces color/token usage via a 3-layer pyramid in chains/rules/enforcement.yaml. Layer 1 (Blocked): hardcoded #hex values, rgb(), rgba(), drop-shadow-*, arbitrary Tailwind values like w-[200px]. Layer 2 (Forbidden): primitive token names text-white, text-black, bg-white, bg-black, and direct primitive imports (import { PRIMARY }). Layer 3 (Preferred): semantic tokens text-error, bg-warning, border-accent. Each layer has increasing strictness from suggestion to blocking action.

## Example

```typescript
Blocked: style={{ color: '#FF0000' }}. Forbidden: className='text-white'. Preferred: className='text-inverse'.
```

## Why

The pyramid provides graduated enforcement — hardcoded values are caught immediately, primitive tokens get warnings, and semantic tokens are the only path that passes all gates.
